title: First Contact
html:
The kappa doesn't move.

Machete at its side. Posture loose, weight settled on both feet, head angled slightly toward the group. Not the posture of something about to swing. The posture of something that has done this before and is not in a hurry.

Around me the group reacts loudly. The loud guy from the protest is already backpedaling. A few others follow him. I stay where I am and let them move past.

The crying woman from the restaurant doesn't move either. She's 8 meters to my left with her arms at her sides, watching the kappa the way she watched the wall for the last two hours.

'Two good variables.'

I put my hand on Soo-Yun's shoulder and move her slightly back and left, out of the direct line between me and the kappa. She doesn't resist.

"Stay here until I signal."

"What's the signal?"

"You'll know it."

The kappa's head tracks the loudest group as they back away toward the treeline. It hasn't raised the machete. It's watching too. I file that.

I know what kappas are from mythology. Water creatures. Strong enough. The shell is real — I can see the wet sheen on it from here, the segmented edges. Mythology didn't cover speed or whether the intelligence was animal or something else. The way it's watching the group suggests something else.

A man breaks from the edge of the crowd and runs at it.

Not the loud guy. Someone smaller, moving fast, with a chunk of rock he must have picked up from the ground. He covers 15 meters in about three seconds and swings.

The kappa sidesteps. One fluid step, the kind that costs it nothing. The rock connects with nothing. The man's momentum carries him two steps past and the kappa turns with him — still not raising the machete, just turning.

The man stumbles, scrambles upright, and runs back toward the group.

The kappa watches him go.

'It's testing us.'

That's not a defensive posture. It's not aggression either. It let the man run. It didn't pursue. It's watching the group as a unit, not picking off individuals. That's tactical, or close enough to it that the difference doesn't matter yet.

---

【"First Encounter: Kappa (Shell-Rank)"】

【"Threat Level: Standard"】

【"Tip: Observe before engaging! Knowledge is survival."】

---

The notification sits in the upper corner of my vision, sharp-edged and pale. I blink. It stays. I look around — other people are touching their faces, looking at things that aren't there. So it's individual. Everyone gets their own screen.

I check what I can find. Stats, some kind of inventory icon, the Threat Level notification still visible. My health is listed as 100. My name is listed. Below it: no class, no title, no abilities. Just numbers in boxes.

The notification is probably accurate about the kappa being standard. If this is a tutorial, they're not opening with the hard version.

The crying woman picks up a branch from the ground. It's about a meter long, solid-looking. She's not holding it like a weapon. More like she hasn't decided yet.

I move toward her.

She clocks me before I'm halfway there. Doesn't move away.

"First time seeing one of these?" I ask, keeping my voice low.

"Yes." Her eyes don't leave the kappa. "You?"

"Yes."

"It let him go."

"I noticed."

Short pause. She shifts her grip on the branch.

"I'm Maya."

"Eric."

"Do you have a plan?"

"Working on it."

The kappa has stopped tracking the crowd. It's watching us now. Me and Maya. The two people who didn't run.

That's a calculation on its end, same as mine on my end. I'm watching it watch me and I think: if it wanted to kill us it would have started when everyone was still bunched up and panicking. It didn't. So it's operating with some kind of goal that isn't immediate maximum casualties.

I don't know if that makes it safer or more dangerous.

"Try to hit the head," I say quietly. "The shell will deflect anything else."

"How do you know that?"

"Mythology. Might be wrong."

She takes that in without complaint.

The kappa takes one step forward.

I pick up a rock from the ground. Not to throw. To have.

It takes another step. The machete is still at its side, blade angled down. It stops about 12 meters out and regards us with the flat attention of something that has looked at a lot of problems before deciding what to do about them.

Then it raises the machete.

Not fast — a slow lift, blade coming up to a guard position. The kind of move that says: now we're doing this.

Maya goes left. I go right. We don't discuss it.

I close the distance in four steps and throw the rock at its face.

The kappa flinches. Just a flinch, head pulling back an inch, but it's enough — its guard comes up to cover and I'm already inside it. I grab the machete arm at the wrist with both hands and put my weight into a turn that uses its own forward lean against it.

It doesn't go down. It's heavier than it looks. But the grip breaks. The machete hits the ground.

Crack! Maya's branch connects with the side of its head. Not full force — a glancing hit, bark splintering on contact — but the kappa stumbles sideways and that's enough room.

I kick the machete away from us both.

The kappa recovers faster than I'd like. It wheels toward Maya with both arms raised and she gets her branch up in time to take the impact on the wood instead of her face. The branch holds. Barely.

I grab its shell from behind. The edges are sharp — I feel it through my palms — but I get purchase on the rim and yank upward and back.

It doesn't flip. That's mythology too, apparently.

But it staggers, and when it does, Maya steps in and drives the end of the branch into the flat of its face. Short and hard, no wind-up.

The kappa goes down on one knee.

I let go of the shell and pick up the machete.

It's heavy. Good steel, if it's steel — bright and clean, no rust. I don't know where a kappa gets a machete but the weight is real.

The kappa looks up at me from one knee. Its expression, if it has one, is unreadable. The flat black eyes don't register pain. They register: status update.

I hold the machete and wait.

After three seconds it lowers its head.

---

【"Encounter Resolved: Kappa (Shell-Rank) — Subdued"】

【"Note: Kappas respect demonstrated dominance. This one will not attack you again."】

【"Reward: Machete acquired. XP pending."】

---

'Respect demonstrated dominance.' I look at the kappa still kneeling in the mud.

So it wasn't a kill scenario. It was a test.

I lower the machete. The kappa rises slowly, takes one measured step back, and turns away into the undergrowth. Gone in four seconds, no sound after the second.

Maya is watching the space where it was.

"That worked," she says. She sounds mildly surprised.

"Mostly."

She looks at her hands. Small cuts from the branch bark. Nothing serious. I have the same on my palms from the shell.

Behind us the crowd has not moved. About 80 people at the edge of the clearing, watching. The loud guy is somewhere in the middle of them with his mouth closed for the first time since we arrived.

Soo-Yun is standing exactly where I put her, arms at her sides, watching me.

I walk back to her. She looks at the machete, then at my hands, then at my face.

"Are you hurt?"

"No."

"Okay." She looks past me at Maya. "Is she a friend?"

"Don't know yet."

Maya follows at a distance that's close enough to be deliberate. She stops about 3 meters away and looks at Soo-Yun.

"Yours?" she asks me.

"No."

"She called you uncle."

"Cultural reflex."

Maya accepts this. She looks at the machete in my hand.

"You should keep that."

"I intend to."

---

【"Tutorial Chapter 1 Objective: Survive 48 Hours"】

【"Time Remaining: 47:18:44"】

【"Tip: Work together or don't. Both are valid strategies."】

---

47 hours. I look at the forest. The trees are tall and dense and whatever is in there, we've seen one sample.

The crowd behind me is starting to recover from the shock of watching two people take down a kappa. Some of them will come to me. Some of them will drift to wherever the loud guy decides to go. Some will go off alone and that's probably the last we'll see of some of them.

I look at the notification. 'Both are valid strategies.' That's not reassurance. That's a design note. Whoever wrote the tutorial wants options to stay open.

"We need water and something for a position," I say to no one in particular. "High ground or a defensible back wall."

Maya nods.

Soo-Yun picks up a stick from the ground. Not a weapon — she's just holding it. She looks at me and doesn't ask if that's okay.

I check the tree line again. The kappa is gone. Whatever else is in there, we haven't seen yet.

Forty-seven hours.

'Start with what you know.'
